speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Read the slide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March the 3rd, 1997. The survivor is Mendel Steinberg. My name is Eileen Molfetas. We're doing the interview in Lauderdale Lakes, Florida, in the United States of America. And the interview is being done in English. My name is Eileen Molfetas. Today is March the 3rd, 1997. I'm doing this interview with Mendel Steinberg in Lauderdale Lakes, Florida, in the United States of America. This interview is going to be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mendel Steinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my name at birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: October the 5th, 1909.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am now 87 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]-- that is near Kiev, between Kiev and [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a city, Dubno. And near the city Dubno was two small cities, [NON-ENGLISH], Murawica, and Lyniv. Over there I was grown up. I was grown up in a very Jewish family, orthodox. You call of Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell the town that you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Hanasel. My mother's name was Chyia Malka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was five-- five brothers and two sisters. One sister, she was living is Israel. Two years ago, she died. She was a very educated woman. She had sugar, she died from this. One sister, with her whole family, Hitler killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the names of your sisters and your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother, his name Getsel. My name is Mendel, [NON-ENGLISH], Hebrew. One sister, Feiga. Hitler killed her and the whole family. One sister, Bunia, and one brother, Chuna, and one brother, Hershel, and one brother, [NON-ENGLISH]. All of them, Hitler killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a businessman, and used to handle with rich people-- farmers, rich farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of business was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything what the people sold, he bought-- cattle, wheat, everything what he was-- he would handle with them. And [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, what did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had seven children, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody else live in your house, besides your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, in Europe, besides your mother and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had a house for myself. I had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nicest house-- but in the city. A luxury house. When I moved-- when the ghetto, the assaults took one German, Schneider. He was the head of all our-- like, the whole area. He was living till he went away when the-- Hitler-- when we start the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, Mr. Steinberg, what you remember about the school that you went to? Do you remember anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. See, I went to school in what-- in yeshiva. And my father, he was thinking he make from me a rabbi, a [SPEAKING HEBREW], you know? And he used to talk with me, when I was young-- Mendel, when you're grown up, when you won't go in shiva, for [INAUDIBLE], you go in shiva in [INAUDIBLE]. My mother had an uncle. If in the areas, people didn't know him-- he was [INAUDIBLE]. He was a rabbi. He was teaching with big boys. When somebody wanted to become a rabbi, he must-- to go-- you go to him, and you must learning. Same with [INAUDIBLE], too. And I was learning by him not quite two years. And my father died from accident-- was 42 years old. And then I came home, and I was with my brother, Getsel, in the same business, what my father-- the same people which my father was handling, we used to handle them. And we became lucky, and we became rich that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg, do you remember when you went to school? Do you remember any anti-semitism in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about what you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it was anti-semitic. It was. They used to put in signs like you should not buying by Jewish people. You make-- you used to call it cooperative, you know, the big stores. And sign was not to buy merchandise by the Jewish people. And it was no good. Like, if I want to go in school to be a doctor, or a lawyer-- you don't-- it's not allowed. No. But was one-- in Dubno-- was one Jew named Vaseman. He was a very rich man-- very rich man. He had three sons. He sent them to Paris, and they became, all three, doctors. In [NON-ENGLISH], he was hiding, and maybe you are still alive. When we left Europe, then he still was in Dubno-- doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, when you were a young boy, any anti-semitism that the children had with you-- the children that you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, for myself, I was honest man. I used to handle with them, as a matter of fact. I-- the only one Jew, but Ukraininan-- he was hating me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like at home, after your father died, before the war? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I for myself?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- I was my brother, together, like I told. I was handling with the same people with my brother-- what-- what-- my father. Night time, four years, I went over there to one was-- [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] is [INAUDIBLE] for everyone who know him. He was [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. He was known a man, but everyone who knows Hebrew, [NON-ENGLISH] he was that [NON-ENGLISH] in Hebrew-- have us three [NON-ENGLISH]. And three friends. The all three was learning in Odessa. [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH]-- gave us-- and I was learning four years, Hebrew, and Russian, and Polish-- night time. [INAUDIBLE] city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, Mr. Steinberg, was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: All important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember all the holidays you celebrated? Can you tell me about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. No question about it. It was a holiday, not like America. It was a holiday in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe them to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you know, no question, everyone shabbat-- came the shabbat, we are talking [INAUDIBLE] shabbats. But the Jewish people-- at the [INAUDIBLE], all was learning. It was Ramban-- the Ramban say, in [NON-ENGLISH], that's our [NON-ENGLISH] Bible, [NON-ENGLISH], and for [NON-ENGLISH] the Ramban said this-- who not to observe-- he who not keeping Shabbat, it's like he nothing, dead. [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] nothing. If he keeps Shabbat-- and even keep the old [INAUDIBLE], anyway, he called [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite Jewish holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish holiday-- every holiday have good things. Like we were talking about Passover, you know. They make Passover clean, and they trying the best. Even the poorest of men, he try the best to make it Passover. [NON-ENGLISH] not [NON-ENGLISH] to eat like it. And other places, no no [INAUDIBLE]. I can say no, [INAUDIBLE] city, it was no one should he not making Passover clean, and to eat Jewish Matzah. Matzah, all kind they eat-- like the women, make good cooks. That's a [NON-ENGLISH], and a holiday, [NON-ENGLISH], that says [INAUDIBLE]. This day, when God gave the Torah for the Jewish people. And that's [NON-ENGLISH], and they did things they you can cook-- milk, and things. The same as Sukkot. Sukkot is a-- Sukkot is not such such a proclaimed holiday, but the orthodox people, you have to eat at Sukkot. Sometimes, it got real [INAUDIBLE], you must run in the house. Those are they-- anyway, happy holiday. And after this, we finish [NON-ENGLISH]-- it's the biggest holiday what can be, by the Jewish people. [NON-ENGLISH]. No, [NON-ENGLISH] supposed-- Jewish people supposed to keep the [NON-ENGLISH]. Oh, but you see, in America, not like this. Everyone, everyone, is [INAUDIBLE]. Even we, in [INAUDIBLE]. At least he's doing that to God. Remember, today is [INAUDIBLE]. God gave the Torah to the Jewish people. At least he have this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg, what do you remember best about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, I remember that he-- like I say, he was an honest man, an orthodox man. a man of [NON-ENGLISH]. I can say [NON-ENGLISH]-- between-- between smart people, learned people, he was-- I was growing up. I was grown up in a house between [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, what was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was the same, like father-- my father was orthodox as she was. The same, no question. They was orthodox, 100%.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was-- she was learning the children. I tried to be a Jew-- I have a [INAUDIBLE] to say pray [INAUDIBLE]. She was orthodox, 100%. And she was learning the children-- my brothers and sisters-- the same as she was. And we were the same. Not the same, [INAUDIBLE]. From generation to generation, not the most, even my brothers-- we till the end, we was-- till Hitler came, we was orthodox, 100%.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you earn your living? What kind of business were you in? Describe it to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I was in business the same what my father was. There was a lot of rich people-- farmers-- used to handle for them. You can know this-- when I went to hiding, nobody want to hide Jewish people. No I went to a Ukrainian, and I told them about this-- I was rich. I say, you and your family, when it came night time, he was scared. He was shushing himself, he said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- he used to call me [NON-ENGLISH], father was Hanasel-- so was in Europe, they call, father's same, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you? Yeah. I say to him, Mr. [NON-ENGLISH]-- the name, was [NON-ENGLISH]-- I'm for you and end, now. You can do with me two things-- you can kill me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he say [NON-ENGLISH], I going kill you. Don't say this. Then, well, you're not going to kill me. Said, you know, I gave you so much money, you know. Should you-- you shouldn't not supposed to work in all your life. And as a matter of fact, I have 100 golden rubles, five-- five rubles a piece. I gave each one [INAUDIBLE] right away. He say, no. I'm not taking it. I'm going to ask my wife, what she will tell of this. He went in the house, took maybe 20 minutes. He came back. And he say, [NON-ENGLISH], my wife told me, a Jew [INAUDIBLE] like you, he must be safe. And I worked with this guy-- wit this guy, 18 months. The last month, [INAUDIBLE] the Polish people kill him. And I was over there, out in the middle field. She came to me, she say, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the Polish people kill my son today.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to take you back to before the war, Mr. Steinberg. And I want you to tell me, when you were in business, were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wife's name, and your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name was Sheindel. My son's name was El. My son was six years when I hide him in the field-- six years old. Night time, I used to carry a little water-- he didn't have to drink it. Anyway, I save him from Hitler. I save him and he-- when I came after the war, he was living in Cleveland. He became successful in business. He was a builder. He became successful. Then all of a sudden, he became cancer, and died. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you, Mr. Steinberg, when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: '42 [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When the war broke out, I was about 28 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- in '42. See-- like, [NON-ENGLISH], I was the first one what I went away for the ghetto. In '42, October, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in the town when the war came?

speaker: interviewee
text: When war came?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the town, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city is now nothing. They took the whole city. They took away the houses, and everything what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how they did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They not supposed to have a permission of this. They-- everyone did what he wants to do. No jury, nobody-- nobody-- nobody stop him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do with you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family he killed. My mother, she was run away together with my two brothers and one sister. She was by a big-shot farmer. No, after this, the boss-- the big farmer-- he, no, say he didn't need us. He say that people are looking for him. No, by brother was no, he did this. No, after, when-- after Hitler came, right away, they kill him. My brother was-- he told everything, and they kill him over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you sent to a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you sent to a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was in ghetto. I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mlynow ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I, for myself, I was working two miles far from the ghetto way for one big-shot man. He was killed, no, see, the manager still was-- of the still farm-- he was called [NON-ENGLISH] I wrote a book, and in the book is everything what I was-- I could put everything in the book. I put-- I was-- worth four times as much [INAUDIBLE] in book. No, I was ready to read for myself-- to write, for myself, a book. And all of a sudden, I became sick, I became a stroke. Then I'm not able to read in the book, to write in the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were working outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the ghetto, two miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man put me in, like I'm a-- [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] mean a gardener. And I had two team people-- the nicest people, whom I want. I took in-- the nicest people-- and between the two team people was the rabbi, a rabbi [NON-ENGLISH] in our city. The name of the rabbi was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was with me in the ghetto. In the ghetto, he was all time, and they kill him. We was working to the last day, till one man came from [NON-ENGLISH] from [NON-ENGLISH], and he told-- this man was the name Schneider. He was used to help for the-- for the-- for the everything, for the [NON-ENGLISH]. They used to give him this gold, and everything-- you know, he used to say everything. It's a [INAUDIBLE] from Hitler, till October the 10, must be kill out all Jewish people in Ukraine. When [INAUDIBLE] told me this, in the morning, we went to work, and we came in field, and we was digging potatoes. Then I say to the people what was working, stop the work. So you know-- they saw something going on. My son came-- he was six years old-- he came for himself in ghetto. And I wrote a letter to my wife, I had over there a hiding place. But I had over there a lot, a lot of gold, silver, dollars, and should take out everything, what she could carry, from the ghetto. And took-- and [NON-ENGLISH] was waiting for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, no, she was killed, from the letter, what I wrote to her. And she didn't do nothing-- she was baking bread at the time. She took two bread. She making bread-- I say, what you say, making bread? I could-- now, I can-- [MUMBLING]. No, everything is left over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my luck it was, I had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] $400 I had hiding in one place, and $400 from second place. The one place, it was-- like Europe, it was the stuffs, it was from brick. Then I-- behind the stuff, I took up the bricks, and I put in over there, in a box, what my grandfather used to keep in tobacco, to smoke. They put in-- I use-- I put in the dollars. And it was too hot. And when I came from the ghetto, I-- then I went over there. I was living over there by a chair-- shoemaker, actually. No, I didn't ask him to do nothing. I took a hammer, and he came to my house, and took out-- and I took out that-- it was on [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't tell him nothing. When I came home, [INAUDIBLE] it was-- for the all dollars, it was pieces-- like small pieces-- and nothing was. No, I was-- no, I didn't want-- I threw it away. I took the same paper, and I was slipping [INAUDIBLE] where I went. Until I came to America. I came to America, and I had-- in Cleveland-- [INAUDIBLE] family-- Dr. Abrams, he was. And my uncle was known [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, Mendel, for the whole thing, and not-- and nothing left you. Then I started to told him what's happened. And I told him, he-- very pieces, what you say. I said, oh yeah, but uncle, I say, it's nothing. No, he had a lawyer, Dr.--a lawyer libre-- he have a brother, a doctor. And he was-- him's lawyer-- my uncle's lawyer. He told him everything. And he wrote a letter to Washington. And he told them to send everything to Washington. The lawyer did this, and he said, in fact, then came a letter. And you-- some $270 he find numbers. He sent me a letter-- $370-- and he sent me a letter after I got home. He [INAUDIBLE], and he send me the money. That's what I left me, from all of fortune.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop now, and we're going to continue this on the next tape, Mr. Steinberg.
